summary: McKenzie describes removing nag screens from his Bingo Card Creator software after realizing they were counterproductive. He had 5-second delays at program start and end, but recognized that his existing conversion mechanism—prompting users when they attempt restricted features—was far more effective. He also added a help menu link to the website that doubles as a tracking mechanism to distinguish registered from unregistered users.
key_points: Nag screens that delay users before they even reach the main screen are counterproductive to conversion
Context-sensitive purchase prompts triggered by restricted actions are a superior sales mechanism to blanket nagging
Tracking user behavior through clever URL routing provides actionable data on software usage
Early-stage software businesses struggle to tell if anyone is actually using the product
money_quotes: With that in place, why annoy people so much that they never get to the main screen?

I have a much, much better sales mechanism built in: every time the user tries to do one of my forbidden actions they get the error message and a gentle prompt that takes them straight to my purchasing screen.
That way I can see if anybody is actually using my software, which is hard to tell now unless they're one of the two people who routinely click the 'purchase this now' button and then do nothing.
key_insight: Friction-based nag screens drive users away before they experience value, while context-sensitive purchase prompts at the moment of need are a far more effective conversion mechanism.
